title: <html>
summary: The root element of an HTML page. Every HTML document must have exactly one <html> element, which wraps all other content.
syntax:
<html lang="en">
  <!-- head and body go here -->
</html>

attributes.name: lang
attributes.description: Specifies the language of the document, e.g. lang="en".

title: <head>
summary: Contains metadata about the document: title, links to stylesheets, character encoding, and more. Nothing inside <head> is displayed on the page.

title: <body>
summary: Contains all the visible content of the web page: text, images, links, and everything the user sees.

title: <h1>
summary: The top-level heading element. Use it for the main title of the page. There should be only one <h1> per page for accessibility and SEO.

title: <h2>
summary: A second-level heading. Use after <h1> to introduce sections within your page.

title: <h3>
summary: A third-level heading. Use for sub-sections within an <h2> section.

title: <h4>
summary: A fourth-level heading. Use sparingly — if you need h4, consider whether your content structure could be simplified.

title: <h5>
summary: A fifth-level heading. Rarely needed; consider restructuring if you reach this level.

title: <h6>
summary: The lowest-level heading. Very rarely used in practice.

Defines a paragraph of text. Browsers automatically add space before and after each

element.
example: Welcome to our website. We hope you enjoy your visit.

title: <a>
summary: Creates a hyperlink to another page, a section of the same page, an email address, or any URL. The href attribute specifies the destination.

attributes.name: href
attributes.description: The URL or path the link goes to.

attributes.name: target
attributes.description: Where to open the link. Use _blank to open in a new tab.

attributes.name: rel
attributes.description: Relationship. Use rel="noopener" with target="_blank" for security.

summary: Embeds an image in the page. The src attribute specifies the image file. The alt attribute provides a text description for accessibility — always include it.

attributes.name: src
attributes.description: Path or URL to the image file.

attributes.name: alt
attributes.description: Alternative text for screen readers and when the image fails to load.

title: <ul>
summary: An unordered (bulleted) list. Contains <li> items. Use when the order of items doesn't matter.

title: <ol>
summary: An ordered (numbered) list. Contains <li> items. Use when the order matters, such as steps in a process.

attributes.name: start
attributes.description: The number to start counting from.

attributes.name: type
attributes.description: Numbering style: 1, A, a, I, or i.

example:
1. Open the file
  2. Edit the HTML
  3. Save and preview

title: <li>
summary: A list item. Must be inside a <ul> (unordered) or <ol> (ordered) list.

attributes.name: value
attributes.description: Sets the number for this item in an <ol> list.

title: <div>
summary: A generic block-level container. It groups content for styling with CSS or targeting with JavaScript. It has no meaning on its own.

attributes.name: class
attributes.description: Assigns one or more CSS class names.

attributes.name: id
attributes.description: A unique identifier for this element.

title: <span>
summary: A generic inline container, used to group text or inline elements for styling with CSS. Unlike <div>, it does not break the flow of text.

title: <section>
summary: Defines a distinct section of a document, such as a chapter or a group of related content. Sections should generally have a heading.

title: <header>
summary: Represents introductory content for its nearest parent section, or the whole page. Typically contains a logo, site title, and navigation.

title: <footer>
summary: Represents the footer for its nearest section or for the whole page. Usually contains copyright, contact links, or site credits.

title: <nav>
summary: Marks a group of navigation links. Screen readers use this to help users skip straight to the main content.

title: <main>
summary: Contains the central content of the page. There should be only one <main> per page. It helps screen readers jump past headers and navigation.

title: <article>
summary: Represents self-contained content that could stand alone, like a blog post, news story, or product listing.
example:
How to Write HTML

HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language...

title: <aside>
summary: Content that is related to the main content but could be removed without losing the main meaning — sidebars, pull quotes, or tips.
example:
Did you know?

The first website went live in 1991.

title: <table>
summary: Creates a data table. Tables should only be used for tabular data — not for page layout. Use CSS Flexbox or Grid for layout instead.

title: <thead>
summary: Groups the header rows of a table. Contains <tr> elements with <th> cells.

title: <tbody>
summary: Groups the body rows of a table. Contains <tr> elements with <td> cells.

title: <tr>
summary: A table row. Contains <th> or <td> cells. Each <tr> represents one horizontal row in the table.

title: <th>
summary: A table header cell. Bold and centred by default. Use scope="col" or scope="row" for better accessibility.

attributes.name: scope
attributes.description: col or row — describes what the header applies to.

attributes.name: colspan
attributes.description: Number of columns this cell spans.

title: <td>
summary: A standard table data cell. Each <td> holds one piece of data in a table row.

attributes.name: colspan
attributes.description: Number of columns this cell spans.

attributes.name: rowspan
attributes.description: Number of rows this cell spans.

title: <form>
summary: Creates an HTML form for collecting user input. The action attribute specifies where data is sent; method specifies how (GET or POST).

attributes.name: action
attributes.description: URL to send the form data to.

title: <input>
summary: Creates an interactive input field. The type attribute determines what kind — text, password, email, checkbox, radio, file, submit, and many more.

attributes.name: type
attributes.description: Type of input: text, email, password, number, checkbox, radio, file, submit, etc.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: The name sent with the form data.

attributes.name: placeholder
attributes.description: Hint text shown when the field is empty.

attributes.name: required
attributes.description: Makes the field mandatory before form submission.

attributes.name: value
attributes.description: The initial value of the field.

title: <textarea>
summary: A multi-line text input field. Use for longer messages or comments. Size is controlled by rows and cols, or by CSS.
example: <textarea name="message" rows="5" placeholder="Write your message here..."></textarea>

title: <select>
summary: Creates a dropdown list. Fill it with <option> elements. Use <optgroup> to group related options.

attributes.name: multiple
attributes.description: Allow selecting more than one option.

title: <option>
summary: Represents one choice inside a <select> dropdown. The value attribute holds the data sent; the text shown is between the tags.

attributes.name: value
attributes.description: The data value submitted with the form.

title: <button>
summary: A clickable button. The type attribute controls behaviour: submit sends a form, reset clears it, button does nothing by default (use with JavaScript).

attributes.name: type
attributes.description: submit (default in forms), reset, or button.

attributes.name: disabled
attributes.description: Prevents the button from being clicked.

title: <label>
summary: Associates descriptive text with a form input. Click the label and the linked input gets focus. Link using the for attribute (matching the input's id).

attributes.name: for
attributes.description: The id of the input this label is for.

title: <link>
summary: Links an external resource to the page. Most commonly used to attach a CSS stylesheet. Goes inside the <head>.

attributes.name: href
attributes.description: Path or URL of the resource.

title: <meta>
summary: Provides metadata about the document. Common uses: setting character encoding, viewport settings for mobile, and page description for search engines.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Type of metadata: viewport, description, author, etc.

example: <meta name="description" content="A great website about HTML.">

title: <title>
summary: Sets the title shown in the browser tab and in search engine results. Goes inside the <head> element.

title: <script>
summary: Embeds or links to JavaScript. Usually placed just before </body> so it loads after the page content. Use the src attribute to link an external file.

attributes.name: src
attributes.description: Path to an external JavaScript file.

attributes.name: defer
attributes.description: Delays script execution until the page has parsed.

title: <style>
summary: Embeds CSS styles directly in the HTML file. Prefer linking an external stylesheet with <link> for larger projects.
Inserts a line break within text. Self-closing. Use sparingly — prefer CSS margin/padding for spacing.

title: <hr>
summary: Draws a horizontal rule (dividing line) between sections of content. Self-closing. Style using CSS border properties.

title: <strong>
summary: Marks text as important, rendering it bold. Screen readers give it added emphasis. Use when the content is genuinely important, not just for visual styling.

title: <em>
summary: Marks text with emphasis, rendering it italic. Screen readers stress the word. Use for genuine emphasis, not just styling.

title: <small>
summary: Renders text at a smaller font size. Typically used for fine print, disclaimers, or copyright notices.

title: <blockquote>
summary: Represents a section quoted from another source. Browsers typically indent it. Use the cite attribute to credit the source.

attributes.name: cite
attributes.description: URL of the source of the quotation.

example: The best way to predict the future is to invent it.

title: <code>
summary: Displays inline code in a monospace font. Use for short code snippets within a paragraph.

title: <pre>
summary: Preformatted text — preserves whitespace, spaces, and line breaks exactly as written. Often used with <code> to display multi-line code blocks.

title: <figure>
summary: Groups self-contained media content such as an image, diagram, or code snippet together with its caption. Often paired with <figcaption>.

title: <figcaption>
summary: Provides a visible caption or legend for a <figure> element. Usually the first or last child of the <figure>.

title: <video>
summary: Embeds a video player. Use controls to show play/pause buttons. The src attribute or <source> child specifies the video file.

attributes.name: controls
attributes.description: Shows the default browser video controls.

attributes.name: muted
attributes.description: Starts muted (required for autoplay in most browsers).

attributes.name: width
attributes.description: Width of the video player in pixels.

example: <video src="intro.mp4" controls width="800">Your browser doesn't support video.</video>

title: <audio>
summary: Embeds an audio player. Include controls to show play/pause buttons. Use <source> child elements for multiple formats.

title: <iframe>
summary: Embeds another web page or resource (such as a YouTube video or Google Map) inside your page.

attributes.name: src
attributes.description: URL of the page to embed.

title: <details>
summary: Creates a disclosure widget — content is hidden by default and revealed when clicked. Pair with <summary> for the visible heading.

attributes.name: open
attributes.description: If present, the details are expanded by default.

example:
What is HTML?

HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language.

title: <summary>
summary: The visible heading for a <details> element. Clicking it toggles the rest of the content open or closed.
